The 90-day supply reduces per-dose costs and incentivizes members to use mail order for ongoing medications. Maintenance medications eligible for mail order include: chronic disease management (diabetes, hypertension, cholesterol), mental health (antidepressants), and pain management (non-opioid). First fills or new medications may be limited to 30-day supplies to assess tolerance before committing to 90-day quantities. Refill timing is automated: the plan mails the next 90-day supply at the 60-day mark unless the member requests a different timing. Members can request early refills (up to one early refill per medication per year) for vacation, travel, or anticipated supply shortage. Controlled substances are not available through mail order except through specialty pharmacy for legitimate opioid maintenance therapy. Members can opt out of mail order and use retail pharmacies with standard 30-day copayments.
formulary_guidelines.tier_structure: Two copayments for 90-day mail order supply: two Tier 1 copays ($30), two Tier 2 copays ($70), two Tier 3 copays ($120), specialty coinsurance applies to 90-day supply
formulary_guidelines.step_therapy_requirements: Step therapy applies to mail order; brand-name drugs subject to same step-therapy rules as retail
formulary_guidelines.quantity_limits: 90-day supply for mail order; 30-day for retail; vacation supply 30 days without additional copayment annually

formulary_guidelines.title: Vacation Supply Rules and Travel Medication Access
formulary_guidelines.content: Members traveling or away from home may request up to a 30-day vacation supply of maintenance medications without triggering early refill limits or additional copayments. The vacation supply rule allows members to obtain a second fill within 30 days for travel purposes, recognizing that members may not have mail order access during travel. Vacation supplies are requested through the pharmacy or online system; typically just a request for 'vacation supply' is sufficient without detailed documentation. Vacation supplies count toward annual fill limits; members are limited to one vacation supply per maintenance medication per calendar year. Controlled substances may have additional documentation requirements (travel itinerary) to prevent misuse. Members traveling internationally should contact the plan in advance for information about pharmacy access in their destination country; in some cases, prescriptions may be transferred to local pharmacies (not covered by U.S. insurance). Members should carry prescriptions and summary documents for medications when traveling.
formulary_guidelines.tier_structure: Vacation supply copayment matches the medication's standard tier copayment
formulary_guidelines.step_therapy_requirements: Vacation supplies subject to same formulary restrictions and step-therapy requirements as regular fills
formulary_guidelines.quantity_limits: Vacation supply up to 30 days; limited to one per medication per calendar year; does not extend annual supply limits

formulary_guidelines.title: Formulary Exception Process for Non-Covered and Specialized Medications
formulary_guidelines.content: Formulary exceptions allow members to obtain non-covered medications or step-therapy exceptions when medically necessary and when alternative formulary options are inappropriate or ineffective. Common exception scenarios include: patient already stable on a non-covered medication (continuation of therapy), documented allergy to all available alternatives, prior trial failure of formulary drugs with documented inadequate response, and medical comorbidities making a non-formulary drug necessary. Exceptions are requested by the prescriber through prior authorization submission or by the member contacting Member Services. Documentation required includes: clinical justification explaining why formulary alternatives are inappropriate or inadequate, patient history of prior therapy attempts, and any relevant clinical parameters supporting the need for the non-covered drug. Exceptions for brand-name drugs typically result in Tier 3 or Tier 4 cost-sharing even if the drug becomes covered. Exceptions are usually granted for 6-12 months and may require periodic re-review if clinical status changes.
formulary_guidelines.tier_structure: Formulary exception cost-sharing varies; brand-name exceptions typically Tier 3 ($60 retail, $150 mail) or Tier 4 (25% coinsurance)
formulary_guidelines.step_therapy_requirements: Formulary exception documentation includes statement that step-therapy agents have been tried and were inadequate or contraindicated
formulary_guidelines.quantity_limits: Exception grants typically specify authorized quantity (e.g., 30-day supply for 90 days duration); refills may require re-authorization

appeals_and_grievances.title: Internal Claim Appeal Process and Timeline
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members have the right to appeal any claim denial, coverage limitation, or benefit determination by submitting an internal appeal to the plan. Claims appeals must be filed within 180 days of the initial denial notice. Standard appeals are reviewed within 30 days of receipt; expedited appeals for urgent or health-risk situations are reviewed within 72 hours (3 business days). To file an appeal, members submit a written request (letter, form, online submission) with their claim number, the reason they believe the denial was incorrect, and any supporting medical records or documentation. A clinical reviewer (nurse or physician) reviews the claim and determines whether coverage should be approved based on the plan's medical policies, formulary, and member's plan benefits. The reviewer issues a written decision explaining the appeal outcome and, if denied, the reason and member's next steps (external review right). Members can request a clinical reviewer override if clinical judgement is questioned.
required_documents: Claim denial notice
Request for appeal form
Medical records supporting medical necessity
Proof of coverage/policy details
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Internal appeal review → Clinical reviewer determination → Written decision → External review if denied → State insurance commissioner if all appeals exhausted

appeals_and_grievances.title: External Independent Review Process for Denied Coverage
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members have the right to independent external review (by a physician not employed by the plan) for any claim denial involving clinical judgment or medical necessity determinations. External reviews are requested after the internal appeal is denied. An independent review organization (IRO) designated by the state conducts the review, selecting a physician reviewer with expertise in the relevant clinical area. The IRO considers all clinical information submitted by the member, treating provider, and plan. The reviewer has 30-45 days to issue a determination regarding whether the denied service was medically necessary. If the external reviewer agrees that coverage is medically necessary, the plan must immediately approve and cover the service. External review determinations are binding on the plan. External review is available at no cost to the member; the plan pays all IRO and reviewer fees. Expedited external reviews are available when the member has an urgent health condition.
required_documents: Denied internal appeal decision
Clinical information and medical records
Treating provider documentation of medical necessity
Any expert opinion supporting medical necessity
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Internal appeal denial → Request external review → IRO review → External determination (binding on plan) → State insurance commissioner if procedural issues

appeals_and_grievances.title: Expedited Appeal Process for Urgent Health Situations
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members and providers can request expedited appeal when the member has an urgent health condition and standard 30-day appeal timelines would jeopardize the member's health. Expedited appeals are appropriate when the member has serious health risks, acute exacerbation of chronic conditions, or impending medical procedures. Expedited appeal requests are submitted by phone, fax, or urgent portal submission. The plan responds within 72 hours (3 business days) with a determination. If the expedited appeal is granted, the plan immediately covers the service. If the expedited appeal is denied, the member is notified of the denial reason and retains the right to file a standard internal appeal and external review. Providers treating the member can contact the plan directly to request expedited review and discuss the urgent clinical situation. Expedited timelines require member or provider certification that standard timelines would jeopardize health.
required_documents: Request for expedited review with urgent health justification
Recent clinical documentation
Provider letter describing urgency and health risk
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Expedited appeal denial → Standard internal appeal → External review → State insurance commissioner

appeals_and_grievances.title: Grievance Filing Process for Service and Coverage Issues
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members can file formal grievances (complaints) about their health insurance coverage, benefits, or service experience separate from claims appeals. Grievances address issues such as: difficulty accessing care, provider network problems, billing disputes, quality of service, customer service failures, and violations of member rights. Grievances must be filed within 180 days of the issue and can be submitted in writing (mail, email, online form), by phone, or in person at a plan office. The plan acknowledges receipt of the grievance within 5 business days and assigns a grievance advocate to investigate. The plan reviews the grievance for potential violations of state insurance regulations or plan policies and contacts relevant parties (provider, billing department, member services) for information. The plan provides written response within 30 days explaining the findings and any remedial actions taken. If the grievance involves a denied benefit or coverage determination, the plan may consolidate it with a claims appeal process.
required_documents: Grievance form or written description of issue
Dates and times of incident
Names of plan employees or providers involved
Copies of relevant correspondence or billing documents
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Grievance filing → Grievance advocate investigation → Written response → State insurance commissioner complaint if unsatisfied → Attorney general office

appeals_and_grievances.title: Provider Appeals for Claim Disputes and Coverage Determinations
appeals_and_grievances.content: Healthcare providers can appeal on behalf of members for denied claims or coverage determinations. Providers submit appeals directly to the plan's provider relations department using the electronic portal, fax, or phone. Provider appeals follow the same timeline as member appeals: standard appeal decisions within 30 days, expedited appeals within 72 hours. Providers include clinical documentation, test results, treatment notes, and clinical justification explaining why the denied service was medically necessary. The plan's clinical reviewer considers provider input alongside member information and plan policies. Providers are notified of appeal decisions simultaneously with members. If an appeal is denied, providers can request peer-to-peer review with the plan's medical director (physician-to-physician discussion) to discuss the clinical case and determination. Peer-to-peer reviews often result in claim reconsideration or coverage approval.
required_documents: Claim information and denial notice
Comprehensive clinical documentation and treatment notes
Clinical justification and medical necessity statement
Any relevant test results or imaging
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Provider appeal → Clinical reviewer → Peer-to-peer review option → Mediator or arbitration if contractual dispute → Litigation if needed

appeals_and_grievances.title: Pharmacy and Formulary Exception Appeal Process
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members and pharmacists can request formulary exceptions (coverage of non-covered medications or step-therapy exceptions) when formulary alternatives are medically inappropriate or ineffective. Pharmacy exception requests are submitted by the treating physician through the pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) portal, fax, or phone call to the prior authorization team. Requests include: patient diagnosis, medication being requested, reason it is medically necessary, documentation of any prior therapy trials and results, and any clinical parameters supporting the need. The PBM pharmacist or clinical pharmacist reviewer processes the exception within 24-48 hours (urgent requests within a few hours). Approval results in coverage of the non-covered drug typically at Tier 3 or Tier 4 cost-sharing. Denial of the exception can be appealed through the standard claims appeal process. High-risk medications (opioids, controlled substances) may require additional documentation and oversight. Approved exceptions typically remain valid for 6-12 months unless clinical status changes.
required_documents: Prescription and patient information
Diagnosis and clinical indication
Documentation of prior medication trials and failure reasons
Prescriber contact information for clarification
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Pharmacy exception denial → PBM appeal → Plan clinical review → External review through standard claims appeals process

appeals_and_grievances.title: Out-of-Network Provider Exception Request and Approval
appeals_and_grievances.content: Members can request out-of-network exceptions when in-network providers are unavailable, cannot provide necessary care, or the member has compelling reasons to seek care from an out-of-network provider. Out-of-network exception requests are submitted to Member Services or Provider Network department with documentation explaining the need (e.g., subspecialty expertise not available in-network, recent relocation out of service area, established patient relationship with out-of-network provider). The plan reviews availability of comparable in-network providers; if none exist or all in-network options are exhausted, the exception may be approved. Approved out-of-network exceptions allow members to see the out-of-network provider at in-network cost-sharing rates (copay/coinsurance), protecting the member from balance billing. Exceptions typically require advance approval before services are rendered; emergency exceptions may be granted retroactively. Out-of-network exceptions are time-limited (3-6 months) or service-limited (specific course of treatment) depending on the reason.
required_documents: Written request explaining need for out-of-network care
Provider information (name, specialty, location)
Documentation that in-network options are unavailable or unsuitable
Provider agreement to in-network rates if exception approved
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Initial exception request → Provider network review → Approval or denial with explanation → Appeal rights if denied

appeals_and_grievances.title: Continuation of Benefits During Appeal Process
appeals_and_grievances.content: When a member appeals a claim denial or seeks review of a coverage determination for ongoing treatment, the plan continues providing the disputed service during the appeal process under specific conditions. Continuation applies only if the member submitted the appeal timely (within 180 days of the denial), the service was initially authorized or rendered in-network, and the appeal involves a question of medical necessity or coverage policy (not a new or experimental service). The plan continues coverage at the same copayment and cost-sharing level as if the service were approved, pending the appeal outcome. If the appeal is ultimately denied, the member may be responsible for repaying the plan for services received during the appeal (retroactive denial). Continuation during appeal is particularly important for ongoing treatments (chemotherapy, dialysis, ongoing therapy) where service interruption would be medically harmful. The plan documents continuation of benefits in writing and communicates the condition that repayment may be owed if the appeal is denied.
appeals_and_grievances.timeline_days: Duration of appeal process (30-45 days standard, 3 days expedited, or longer for external review)
required_documents: Appeal request and claim information
Confirmation that service is ongoing and medically necessary
Documentation supporting appeal
appeals_and_grievances.escalation_path: Continuation granted during appeal → Appeal decision issued → If denied, repayment demand issued → Member can dispute repayment through grievance process
